title: Lecture 5 2014T1
body:
Lecture 5 – Binomial Trees

Hull Chapter 12  One-step binomial model and no arbitrage argument

• Let’s start with a very simple model • Between time 0 and time T the stock price can either go up by a factor u or go down by a factor d • No other outcomes are possible • We will not specify the probabilities of up or down movements yet • Besides the stock, there is a risk-free asset paying a continuously compounded interest rate of r

• We typically describe the possible stock prices with a binomial tree

Using the binomial model to price a derivative

• Let’s introduce an option written on the stock • For now, consider only European style, e.g., options that can only be exercised at time T • Denote the value of the option by f • The option has a payoff function Ф, such that 𝑓𝑢 = Ф(𝑆𝑢 ) 𝑓𝑑 = Ф(𝑆𝑑 ) • Our tree, including the option, looks like below

Replicating the risk-free asset

• To price the option, i.e., to find 𝑓0 , we will replicate the risk-free asset using a combination of the stock and the option • The risk-free asset grows by 𝑒 𝑟𝑇 over the period • If we can form a portfolio of options and stocks that is also a risk-free asset, the portfolio must grow by the same rate (by no-arbitrage) • Let’s buy Δ stocks and short one option • Our portfolio value, Π, will be ΔS - f • In the tree, our portfolio will look as follows

Replicating the risk-free asset

• Since we want our payoff to be risk-free, we must have the same payoff in both the up- and the down-state • That is Π𝑢 = Δ𝑆0 𝑢 – 𝑓𝑢 = Δ𝑆0 𝑑 – 𝑓𝑑 = Π𝑑 • Solve the equation for Δ, i.e. the appropriate number of stocks to buy

• The value of the portfolio at time T is Δ𝑆0 𝑢 – 𝑓𝑢 (or, if you prefer, Δ𝑆0 𝑑 – 𝑓𝑑 ) with probability one. This is a risk-free portfolio as it has the same payoff in the 2 states. • The value of the portfolio at time 0 is Δ𝑆0 – 𝑓0 • At the risk-free rate this will grow to (Δ𝑆0 – 𝑓0 )𝑒 𝑟𝑇 by time T • We have two expressions for the value of the portfolio at time T, which must be equal  Replicating the risk-free asset

• By no-arbitrage, the value of our portfolio at time T must equal its value at time 0 times 𝑒 𝑟𝑇 • Solve the resulting equation for 𝑓0

• Since everything on the RHS is known, we have successfully priced the option. • We’ll do a concrete example shortly

Replicating the risk-free asset and risk-neutral pricing

• Recall that we already have an expression for Δ • Let’s substitute that in to get a neater pricing equation

𝒆𝒓𝑻 −𝒅 • The risk-neutral probabilities 𝒑 = allows us to price any assets by discounting future 𝒖−𝒅 −𝒓𝑻 cash-flows at 𝒆 . 7  Risk-Neutral Pricing of the Underlying Asset

• Let’s try to price the stock by using our new-found risk-neutral probability

𝐸 𝑄 (𝑆𝑇 ) 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇

Risk-Neutral Pricing of the Derivative

𝑒 −𝑟𝑇 −𝑑 • Let’s try to price a derivative using the risk-neutral probabilities: p = 𝑢−𝑑

𝐸 𝑄 (𝑓𝑇 ) 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇

= 𝑓0 • In fact, we can price any asset with the pricing equation:

𝑓0 = 𝐸 𝑄 (𝑓𝑇 ) 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇

where 𝑓 can be a stock, a derivative, a forward contract.  Pricing a European Call option

• Let’s consider a concrete example and price a European call option using the binomial model • Recall that the payoff function for a European call is • Ф(𝑆𝑇 ) = max(𝑆𝑇 − 𝐾, 0) • To be even more concrete, let’s consider a specific current stock price of 𝑆0 = 100 and a specific strike price of K = 107 • Let’s further specify u = 1.25 and d = 0.8, i.e. 𝑆𝑢 = 𝑆0 𝑢 = 100 ∙ 1.25 = 125 𝑆𝑑 = 𝑆0 𝑑 = 100 ∙ 0.8 = 80 • Finally, let’s assume a time-to-maturity of 1 year and risk-free rate of r = 9.5% • The resulting tree is

Pricing a European Call option

• Now buy Δ stocks and sell one call option • Pick Δ so that the portfolio payoff is risk-free

• Our portfolio will be worth

Pricing a European Call option

• We can use the delta approach to calculate the price of the option using:

−𝑟𝑇 −𝑟𝑇 𝑓0 = ∆0 𝑆0 1– 𝑢𝑒 + 𝑓𝑢 𝑒

−0.095 −0.095 = 0.4 ∙ 100 1 – 1.25𝑒 + 18𝑒

= 10.9 • We could also have used:

= ∆0 𝑆0 − ∏𝑢 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇

• The price of the European call option is $10.9

• This is a strict no-arbitrage argument relying on a replicating portfolio, just like when we were pricing forward contracts  Pricing the Stock with Real World Probabilities

• Suppose that we now want to value the stock, 𝑆0 , but using real world probabilities.

• We denote the “true” probability of an increase in the stock price 𝑝∗ , and ∗ hence that the probability of a decrease is (1 − 𝑝 )

• The expected stock price is then

∗ ∗ 𝐸(𝑆𝑇 ) = 𝑝 𝑆0 𝑢 + (1 − 𝑝 )𝑆0 𝑑

• A risk-averse investor would value the stock at

−𝐷𝑇 ∗ ∗ −𝐷𝑇 𝑆0 = 𝐸 𝑆𝑇 𝑒 = [𝑝 𝑆0 𝑢 + (1 − 𝑝 )𝑆0 𝑑] 𝑒

where 𝑫 is the required rate of return (CAPM).

Given the information above and assuming that the time step is 3 months and r=12%, what is the risk-neutral probability of being in a up state after 3 months?

Given that p=0.6523, what is the value of the call option with 3 months to maturity and strike price K=21?

Solving a more realistic model: 2 Steps

• The assumption that the stock can take only two values at time T is restrictive. • Let’s make the model slightly more realistic by introducing one more step. • At any point in time the stock can move either up or down by some factor u or d, at any given step in the tree. • We assume that u*d=1 • Δ𝑡 = 𝑇/2 and the risk-neutral probability of an up move is: • The resulting two-step binomial tree is:

Finding the Risk-Neutral Probability

35 35

• Each time step is 3 months • r=5%, u=1.2, d=1/u=0.8333 • What is the probability of a up move in 3 months?

Parametrizing the tree

■ Binomial trees are frequently used to approximate the movements in the price of a stock or other asset

■ In each small interval of time the stock price is assumed to move up by a proportional amount u or to move down by a proportional amount d.

■ How are we going to choose the parameters u and d?

■ We choose the tree parameters u and d so that the tree gives correct values for the mean & standard deviation of the stock price changes in a risk-neutral world

erΔt = pu + (1– p)d σ2Δt = pu2 + (1– p)d 2 – [pu + (1– p)d ]2

Parametrizing the tree

■ For a small Δt, a solution to the equations is:

Parametrizing the tree

■ We know the value of the option at the final nodes

■ We work back through the tree using risk-neutral valuation to calculate the value of the option at each node, testing for early exercise when appropriate.

■ One-Step Binomial Model

where

Risk-Neutral Valuation of European Options

• Since the risk-neutral probabilities remain constant throughout the tree, it is straightforward to calculate the risk-neutral expectation of the stock price at time T • The probability of ending up in each node (with the middle node being reachable via two paths) is

• The risk-neutral expectation of the terminal derivative price, 𝐸 𝑄 (𝑓𝑇 ), is

• And we can easily get its present value by discounting at the risk-free rate

Example: Pricing an American put option

• Binomial trees are the preferred way of valuing American put options, since they allow us to take potential early exercise into account • Let’s use a two-step binomial tree to price an American put option given the following data

• It follows that the risk-neutral probabilities are

Example: Pricing an American put option

• Set up the tree, and start using backwards induction

• From the top second node, the derivative will be worth 0 at time T regardless of how the stock moves. Therefore: 𝑓𝑢 = 0. • If we don’t exercise the option in the bottom node of the second time step, its value would be given by

• If we do exercise the option, we can immediately cash in its intrinsic value):

Example: Pricing an American put option

• Since we are better off exercising the put option in the bottom node of the second time step, its value is 𝑓𝑑 = 23 • Now, we can work back to the first node. The value if we don’t exercise the option is

• If we exercised the option, we would get

• We are clearly better off keeping the option, and the full tree becomes
